text: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) was launched on December 25, 2021, aboard an Ariane 5 rocket from the Guiana Space Centre in French Guiana. It is the largest optical telescope in space.
JWST orbits the Sun near the second Lagrange point (L2), about 1.5 million kilometers from Earth, rather than orbiting Earth directly like the Hubble Space Telescope.
JWST's primary mirror is 6.5 meters in diameter and is composed of 18 hexagonal gold-coated beryllium segments. This is significantly larger than Hubble's 2.4-meter mirror.
The telescope primarily observes in the infrared spectrum, which allows it to see through cosmic dust and observe some of the earliest galaxies formed after the Big Bang.
JWST's instruments must be kept extremely cold, around -233 Celsius, to function correctly, which is why the telescope has a large five-layer sunshield roughly the size of a tennis court.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched in 1990 aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery and orbits roughly 547 kilometers above Earth.
Hubble primarily observes in visible and ultraviolet light, complementing JWST's infrared capabilities; the two telescopes are often used together for multi-wavelength studies.
The total cost of the JWST program was approximately $10 billion, making it one of the most expensive science missions ever undertaken.
NASA partnered with the European Space Agency (ESA) and the Canadian Space Agency (CSA) to build and operate JWST.
Servicing missions to repair or upgrade JWST in person are not currently planned, unlike Hubble, which was serviced by astronauts five times because it orbits much closer to Earth.
